titleStmt.title: Letter from Andrew Philip Magill to William Field, 19 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Andrew Philip Magill

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Andrew Philip Magill (1871-1941) the Private Secretary for the Chief Crown Secretary of Ireland, Augustine Birrell (1850-1933) to the M.P. William Field (June 1843 – 29 April 1935). In the letter Magill informs Field that Birrell acknowledged his previous letter. The Under-Secretary, Sir Matthew Nathan, has written a number of comments on the letter in reference to the subject of Field's correspondence regarding a raid on the office of the Gaelic Press, a complaint by Field about a member of parliament and the case of two boys who had been arrested.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/7801/3

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
18th April, 1916. Dear Mr. Field, I am directed by Mr. Birrell to acknowledge the
receipt of your letter of the 14th instant, and
enclosures, which will have attention. Yours faithfully, (Sd.) A.P.M. U.S.
Mr Field mixes up 3 things
I. As to the Gaelic Press — I don't think we need
take any notice of this.
II. Kenny's advertising agency. This letter to Mr.
Field is marked 'private' & it is just as well. I
wd. be inclined to say that this was a matter for the
A.G. & that in any event the C.S. must decline to
take any notice of a letter making
a wholly unwarranted attack on a
prominent official in whom the C.S.
has the greatest confidence
III. The Mulligans. This is probably an
industrial school case. We are replying to a
general question on the subject of children sent to
Indus. Schools but it might be well to send a reply re the Mulligans
A.P.M. William Field, Esq., M.P.,
16, Booterstown Avenue,
DUBLIN.
